overall_topic: Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers :     Occupational Outlook Handbook: :     U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
questions: Within the same federal occupational outlook system that profiles software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers, identify the managerial occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations and is projected to grow at the same percentage rate (2024–2034) as that developer group. For this managerial role, provide its 2024 median annual pay, typical required work experience in a related occupation, projected annual average openings over the decade, and total employment change from 2024 to 2034. Additionally, name the relevant academic field-of-degree category listed in the system that aligns most directly with the typical educational background for this occupation.

In U.S. federal labor statistics, there is a combined occupational category that includes professionals who design computer applications or programs as well as those who identify defects and test those applications for quality. For this occupation group, provide the following: the 2024 median annual wage for each of its two primary role types, the total number of jobs in 2024, the projected percentage employment growth from 2024 to 2034, the estimated annual average number of job openings over the decade, the projected numeric employment change over that period, and the typical entry-level education required. Summarize all figures clearly and distinguish between the two role types where pay differs.

Within the federal labor statistics occupation group that includes professionals who create or support computer applications, systems, and networks—and that is projected to have about 317,700 openings per year on average from 2024 to 2034—identify the following:

(1) The specific occupation in this group whose core duty is designing computer applications or programs. State its 2024 median annual pay and its projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034.

(2) A related management occupation that plans, coordinates, and directs computer-related activities in organizations. For this role, provide the typical amount of related work experience required for entry and its 2024 median annual pay.

(3) The academic major field that directly aligns with preparing workers for these types of careers. Name this field and briefly describe the type of labor market and occupational information that official field-of-degree pages provide about graduates.

Be specific and support each part with the most recent nationally reported figures.

In the United States, there is a major technology occupation group that encompasses professionals who design computer applications and programs as well as those who systematically identify defects and ensure software quality before release. For this combined occupation category, what are the most recent reported median annual wages (specifying the figures separately for the design-focused role and the quality-focused role), the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, the typical entry-level education required, and the average number of projected annual openings over the decade?

In the United States, there is a broad occupational group that includes professionals who design and build computer applications as well as those who systematically test those applications to identify defects and ensure quality. For this combined occupational category, report the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the total number of jobs in 2024, the typical entry-level education required, and the average number of annual openings expected over the decade. Additionally, provide the separate median annual wages (as of May 2024) for (1) the professionals who design and develop software and (2) the professionals who focus on quality assurance analysis and testing. Present all figures clearly and distinguish between the two wage amounts.

Identify the U.S. occupation group that includes professionals who both design computer applications or programs and those who test and identify defects in those applications before release. For this combined occupational category, report the following: the 2024 median annual pay for each of the two primary roles within the group, the typical entry-level education required, the projected employment growth rate for 2024–2034, the estimated number of jobs in 2024, and the average number of projected annual openings over the decade. Present all figures clearly and distinguish pay information between the two roles.

rounds.answer_a:
The managerial occupation in the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook (OOH)—the federal system profiling software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers—is Computer and Information Systems Managers. This role plans, coordinates, and directs organizations’ computer-related activities and shares the 23% 2024–2034 growth rate of the developer group [1]. Its 2024 median annual pay is $164,070 [1]. Typical required work experience in a related occupation is 5 years or more; common prior roles include software developer, IT systems analyst, or network administrator, as these positions build the technical expertise needed to oversee complex computer initiatives [1]. Projected annual average openings over the decade are 37,400, a figure that includes both new job creations driven by digital transformation and replacements for workers retiring or moving to other fields [1]. Total employment change from 2024 to 2034 is an increase of 88,500, fueled by businesses’ growing reliance on cloud computing, cybersecurity, and data analytics [1]. The academic field-of-degree category most directly aligned with its typical educational background is Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services, though some employers also prefer candidates with a master’s in business administration (MBA) with an information systems concentration [1].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024). Computer and Information Systems Managers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) groups computer application designers and quality testing professionals into the “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” occupational category [1]. Its two primary roles have distinct 2024 median annual wages: software developers (who design applications/programs) earn $124,200, while quality assurance (QA) analysts and testers (who identify defects and test applications) earn $92,270 [1]. The group’s total 2024 job count is 1,858,500 [1]. BLS projects a 21% employment growth rate from 2024 to 2034, far faster than the average 2% for all occupations [1]. Over this decade, the estimated annual average number of job openings is 158,600, fueled by new job creation and replacement needs for workers leaving the field [1]. The projected numeric employment change is a net gain of 399,900 jobs [1]. Both primary roles require a typical entry-level education of a bachelor’s degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related field, though some employers may accept equivalent experience or associate degrees for specific QA roles [1].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.answer_b:
The occupation described comprises **Software Developers and Quality Assurance (QA) Testers**, typically classified under software development occupations in federal labor statistics.

**2024 Median Annual Wages:**
- **Software Developers (applications and systems):** $98,670[4]
- **QA Analysts/Testers:** Included in Software Developers category with comparable median salary

**Employment Metrics (2024-2034):**
- **Total jobs (2024):** 121,200[4]
- **Projected employment change:** -6% decline (approximately -7,200 jobs)[4]
- **Annual average job openings:** 5,500 per year[4]
- **Typical entry-level education:** Bachelor's degree[4]

**Key Context:**
Despite the projected 6 percent employment decline, approximately 5,500 job openings are expected annually over the decade, driven entirely by replacement demand from workers transitioning to other occupations or retiring.[4] The median wage of $98,670 reflects employment data from May 2024.[4]

The decline reflects automation and offshoring trends in routine programming work, though demand for specialized developers in emerging technologies remains competitive. The sustained job openings despite overall employment decline indicate significant turnover within the field rather than absolute elimination of positions. Entry-level positions typically require a bachelor's degree in computer science or related discipline.[4]

## References

[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Computer Programmers: Occupational Outlook Handbook. Occupational Employment and Wages, May 2024.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) group with an average 317,700 annual job openings 2024–2034 is Computer and Information Technology Occupations [1]. (1) The specific role focused on designing computer applications or programs is Software Developers, Applications [2]. Its 2024 median annual pay is $124,200, and its projected 2024–2034 employment growth rate is 21%, far faster than the 2% average for all occupations [2]. (2) A related management role is Computer and Information Systems Managers, which plans, coordinates, and directs organizational computer-related activities [3]. Entry into this role typically requires 5 or more years of related work experience (e.g., in software development, IT analysis, or network administration) [3], and its 2024 median annual pay is $164,070 [3]. (3) The direct aligning academic major is Computer Science [4]. Official BLS field-of-degree pages for Computer Science provide graduates with data including median earnings at different career stages, employment rates across key industries, top occupational placements for degree holders, and how varying degree levels (bachelor’s vs. master’s) influence job access and compensation, enabling students and career advisors to evaluate field viability and target strategic career paths [4].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Computer and Information Technology Occupations. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/home.htm
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Computer and Information Systems Managers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm
[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Field of Degree: Computer and Information Sciences and Support Services. https://www.bls.gov/emp/chart-university-majors.htm
rounds.answer_b:
**(1) Software Developers** are the primary occupation whose core duty is designing computer applications and programs within the Computer and Information Technology Occupations group.[1] The 2024 median annual wage for software developers was $120,730.[1] Employment is projected to grow 10 percent from 2024 to 2034, exceeding the average occupation growth rate.[1]

**(2) Computer and Information Systems Managers** plan, coordinate, and direct computer-related activities in organizations.[2] This role typically requires **3 to 5 years of related work experience** for entry.[2] The 2024 median annual wage for computer and information systems managers was $159,010.[2]

**(3) Computer Science** is the academic major field that directly aligns with preparing workers for these careers.[3] Official field-of-degree pages provide critical labor market information including typical occupations held by graduates, wage data by occupation, and employment trends within the field.[3] These resources help prospective students understand long-term career outcomes and earning potential across various computer science specializations.[3]

The broader Computer and Information Technology Occupations group, which encompasses software developers, quality assurance analysts, and related roles, is projected to generate approximately 317,700 job openings annually through 2034.[1] This reflects substantial replacement demand and growth opportunities despite automation pressures in routine programming tasks. The progression from software developer roles to management positions typically requires the noted experience level, with significant salary advancement to management positions. Computer Science degree programs increasingly emphasize both theoretical foundations and practical skills aligned with industry demand, supported by official labor statistics tracking graduate outcomes across different occupational pathways.[3]

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Software Developers: Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024.

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Computer and Information Systems Managers: Occupational Outlook Handbook, 2024.

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. National Center for Education Statistics - Computer Science Field-of-Degree Labor Market Information, 2024.

Width/Completeness: Both fail multiple checklist items. Neither provides the correct 2024 median pay ($131,450) or growth rate (15%) for Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Both provide incorrect median pay for Computer and Information Systems Managers (should be $171,200). Agent A correctly states "5 or more years" of related experience, while Agent B incorrectly states "3 to 5 years" (should be 5 years or more). Both incorrectly name the academic field as "Computer Science" instead of "Computer and information technology." Thus, both significantly fail the WIDE aggregation requirement.

Presentation & UX: Both are reasonably well-structured, with clear numbering and citations. Agent B uses slightly stronger formatting (bold headers), while Agent A provides more detailed citation formatting and correctly states the experience requirement. However, these are marginal differences.

Because BOTH agents contain multiple major factual inaccuracies across required data points (wages, growth rates, and field name), this constitutes a Low Quality Tie under the rubric.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS)’s “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” category encompasses the two specified technology roles [1]. Its 2024 median annual wages differ by focus: the design-focused software developers earn $124,200, while the quality-focused QA analysts and testers earn $92,270 [1]. The category’s total number of jobs in 2024 is 1,858,500 [1]. BLS projects a 21% employment growth rate for 2024–2034, which is vastly faster than the 2% average growth rate for all U.S. occupations [1]. The typical entry-level education required is a bachelor’s degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related field, though some employers may accept associate degrees or equivalent professional experience for certain QA roles [1]. Over the 2024–2034 decade, the average number of projected annual job openings is 158,600, driven by both new job creation and the need to replace workers who leave the occupation [1].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.answer_b:
**Software Developers and QA Testers Combined Occupation Group**

The occupation encompasses Software Developers and Software Quality Assurance Analysts, key roles in application design and software quality verification.[5]

**Median Annual Wages (May 2024):**
- **Software Developers/Programmers:** $98,670[5]
- **QA Analysts/Testers:** Wage data not separately delineated in current federal statistics; typically lower than developers but part of same occupational cluster

**Employment and Projections (2024-2034):**
- **Total jobs (2024):** 121,200[5]
- **Projected growth rate:** -6% decline[5]
- **Numeric employment change:** -7,200 jobs[5]
- **Annual average job openings:** 5,500 positions per year[5]

**Entry-Level Education Required:** Bachelor's degree[5]

**Context:**
The projected decline reflects automation in routine programming tasks and increased offshoring. However, the sustained 5,500 annual openings indicate significant replacement demand from worker transitions and retirements.[5] Specialized developer roles in emerging technologies—artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and cybersecurity—remain in higher demand and command premium compensation. QA positions, while often lower-paid than development roles, benefit from increased emphasis on software quality and testing rigor in enterprise environments. The bachelor's degree requirement remains standard across both roles, though some positions accept candidates with relevant certifications or extensive portfolio experience.[5]

## References

[5] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024). Computer Programmers: Occupational Outlook Handbook. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) groups software design and development professionals with quality assurance (QA) analysts and testers into the unified “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” occupational category [1]. As of May 2024, these two core roles have notable median annual wage differences: those focused on designing and building computer applications earn $124,200, while professionals who systematically test applications to identify defects and ensure quality prior to release earn $92,270 [1]. The category’s total number of jobs in 2024 is 1,858,500 [1]. BLS projects a robust 21% employment growth rate for the group between 2024 and 2034, a pace far faster than the 2% average growth rate for all U.S. occupations combined [1]. Over this 10-year period, the average number of projected annual job openings is 158,600, a figure driven by both substantial new job creation and the need to replace workers who leave the occupation for retirement, career changes, or other reasons [1]. The typical entry-level education required for both roles is a bachelor’s degree in computer science, software engineering, or a closely related field, though some employers may accept associate degrees or demonstrated equivalent professional experience for certain specialized QA positions [1].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.answer_b:
**Software Developers and QA Analysts: Combined Occupation Group**

The combined occupational category encompasses both **Software Developers** and **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers**.

**Employment and Projections (2024–2034):**
- **Total jobs (2024):** 121,200[4]
- **Projected growth rate:** -6% decline[4]
- **Numeric employment change:** -7,200 jobs[4]
- **Average annual job openings:** 5,500 positions per year[4]
- **Entry-level education:** Bachelor's degree[4]

**Median Annual Wages (May 2024):**
- **Software Developers:** $98,670[4]
- **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers:** Separate wage data not distinctly reported in May 2024 federal statistics; historically lower than developer wages but part of the integrated software development occupational cluster

**Context:**
Federal labor statistics do not consistently separate QA tester wages from broader software developer classifications in recent reports. The -6% decline reflects automation and offshoring pressures in routine programming and testing tasks. Despite overall employment contraction, 5,500 annual openings result primarily from replacement demand as workers retire or transition to other roles.[4] The bachelor's degree requirement applies standardly across both design and quality assurance positions, though some roles accept relevant certifications and portfolio experience as alternatives.[4]

## References

[4] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Computer Programmers: Occupational Outlook Handbook. Occupational Employment and Wages, May 2024.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) designates the “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” group as the occupational category encompassing professionals who design computer applications/programs and those who test for defects [1]. 2024 median annual pay differs for the two primary roles: software design and development professionals earn $124,200, while quality assurance analysts and testers earn $92,270 [1]. The group’s total number of jobs in 2024 is 1,858,500 [1]. BLS projects a 21% employment growth rate for 2024–2034, a pace far faster than the 2% average for all U.S. occupations [1]. Over the decade, the average number of projected annual job openings is 158,600, driven by new job creation and the need to replace workers leaving the field [1]. The typical entry-level education required is a bachelor’s degree in computer science, software engineering, or a related field, though some employers may accept associate degrees or equivalent professional experience for certain specialized QA roles [1].

## References
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024, September). Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers. Occupational Outlook Handbook. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm
rounds.answer_b:
**Software Developers and Quality Assurance Analysts Combined Group**

The combined occupational category comprises **Software Developers** and **Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers**, classified under federal computer and mathematical occupations.[3][5]

**2024 Median Annual Wages:**
- **Software Developers/Programmers:** $98,670[5]
- **QA Analysts and Testers:** Separate May 2024 wage data not individually reported in current federal statistics

**Employment and Projections (2024–2034):**
- **Total jobs (2024):** 121,200[5]
- **Entry-level education:** Bachelor's degree[5]
- **Projected growth rate:** -6% decline[5]
- **Numeric employment change:** -7,200 jobs[5]
- **Average annual job openings:** 5,500 positions per year[5]

**Key Context:**
Federal labor statistics do not consistently delineate separate median wages for QA testers versus software developers in recent reports, though QA roles historically command lower compensation than development positions. The projected 6 percent decline reflects automation and offshoring trends affecting routine programming and testing tasks. Despite overall employment contraction, the 5,500 annual openings result primarily from replacement demand as workers retire or transition to other occupations.[5] The bachelor's degree remains the standard entry requirement across both roles, though alternative credentials and demonstrated portfolio experience may qualify candidates for certain positions.[5]

## References

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2023). Software developers was the largest computer and mathematical occupation in May 2022. Occupational Employment and Wages.

[5] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Computer Programmers: Occupational Outlook Handbook. Occupational Employment and Wages, May 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the occupational group as “Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers” and keeps all statistics tied to that group. Agent B names a related category but pulls data from the Computer Programmers occupation (e.g., 121,200 jobs, -6% growth), which is a different BLS entity—this is a DEEP failure. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides all required fields (separate median pay for both roles, education, growth rate, jobs, annual openings), but most figures are numerically incorrect versus the checklist. Agent B not only provides incorrect figures, but also fails to report the required separate QA median wage and gives entirely mismatched employment statistics—this is a WIDE failure as well.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent B has slightly better formatting with bullets and headers. However, the magnitude of factual errors and use of the wrong occupational dataset severely harms reliability. Agent A, despite incorrect numbers, at least stays within the correct occupational framework and answers every requested component clearly.

Because Agent A is flawed but remains logically aligned with the correct occupation while Agent B fails both entity alignment and key data requirements, Agent A is BETTER (not MUCH_BETTER due to its own factual inaccuracies).
